rationales.stimulation: He keeps circling back to the deal because of "the feeling of standing at the edge of something that does not have a guaranteed outcome," showing attraction to uncertainty and challenge.
rationales.security: He knows the "smart move" is to "park the capital" and preserve breathing room, yet he is pulled toward a riskier path that would undermine that stability.

rationales.stimulation: He is already "half-committed" and Ingrid warns him not to be "chasing the feeling again," which points to being drawn by excitement and uncertainty.
rationales.security: He actively runs "downside scenarios" and frames the worst case as "recoverable," showing concrete attention to protecting against risk before moving.

rationales.hedonism: He describes enjoying "Valentine's dinner," not talking about business, and wanting to "just walk along the water with Ingrid," all centered on present pleasure and ease.
rationales.benevolence: He says yes to spending weeks in Gotland with Ingrid's mother and values being present with Ingrid, showing care for close relationships over pure work focus.

rationales.stimulation: He explicitly rejects "the feeling of the leap" and says he should not be "chasing it right now," turning away from novelty and risk-seeking.
rationales.security: He passes on the deal because "the responsible move" is to protect reserves, attend to existing companies, and preserve the solid rhythm he and Ingrid have.

rationales.stimulation: He says instability once felt like "fuel" but now "just feels like a threat," showing active discomfort with uncertainty rather than attraction to it.
rationales.security: He spends the day restructuring and the core distress is that he had grown "comfortable with the idea of things being stable and now the ground shifted," highlighting a strong drive to restore predictability.
He focuses on keeping operations running by finding "a replacement client" and diversifying the pipeline, which directly supports continuity and stability.
rationales.benevolence: He works on-site because "the founder needed to see someone calm in the room" and notices Ingrid's relief, showing active support for close others rather than detached oversight.

rationales.stimulation: He feels that "good shape" is "treading water" and keeps checking for something that will make tomorrow feel different, showing restlessness with maintenance and sameness.

rationales.hedonism: He says the day out, walking for hours and eating pannenkoeken, was "exactly what I needed" and he left the phone away, prioritizing simple enjoyment and relief in the moment.

rationales.security: Although the new concept sparks interest, he deliberately questions whether that instinct is "the problem" and limits himself to "just looking," showing caution against overcommitting.

rationales.stimulation: He is up at "one in the morning" building a model because the deck "grabs" him, showing clear excitement for a new, uncertain opportunity.
rationales.security: He closes the laptop, recalls the Antwerp scare, and says "I do not want to stretch us thin again" because "the reserves are there for a reason," prioritizing protection and restraint.

rationales.stimulation: He admits "I miss the feeling of building something from nothing" and recognizes that urge is what is pulling him toward the deal beyond the formal business case.

rationales.self_direction: Instead of forcing himself into one role, he starts looking for "a version of this where I do not have to choose," showing an effort to define his own path rather than accept a false binary.

rationales.hedonism: He says his "eyes hurt" and he "skipped lunch entirely," subordinating comfort and immediate wellbeing to work demands.
rationales.achievement: He spends hours fixing the warehouse issue by writing "a detailed process document" and taking on both his and Camilo's responsibilities to keep performance on track.
Being selected "out of four hundred applicants" matters because it is "external proof" that the company is real, showing strong orientation toward recognized success and validation.
He tracks that their retention was "the second highest in the room" and insists the point is to "win something," showing a strong drive to outperform peers.
rationales.power: He treats the cohort as a status ranking and says "you perform confidence" with mentors, reflecting concern with leverage, standing, and how he is perceived in the hierarchy.

rationales.achievement: He highlights that revenue is up 14% and CAC is down, focusing on concrete performance metrics and visible progress.
He defends skipping brunch because accelerator prep is "a step" toward investors and says "this is the thing" he is building, clearly prioritizing advancement.
rationales.benevolence: Laura asks for time together, but he keeps centering slides and even checks email "under the table," showing neglect of a close relationship's needs.

rationales.achievement: He does not want merely a "strong position"; he wants to be "the one they point to" from the cohort, showing a direct drive for standout success.
rationales.power: He says that standout status would create "credibility" and "leverage" that changes investor conversations before he pitches, emphasizing influence and reputation as assets.

rationales.achievement: He misses his cousin's wedding because the pitch competition takes priority, putting venture progress ahead of family obligations.
rationales.tradition: Skipping a family wedding and disappointing his tia and mother conflicts with maintaining important familial rituals and expectations.
rationales.benevolence: He knowingly disappoints close family members for work, showing a willingness to let their emotional needs take second place.

rationales.achievement: He fixates on placing "Second. Out of sixty companies" and the judges calling their presentation "the most polished," centering competitive performance and recognition.

rationales.hedonism: He has worked from "6 AM" to nearly midnight and says Laura sent dinner because she knew he "wouldn't eat otherwise," showing self-denial of basic comfort for work.
rationales.achievement: He frames the investor meetings as "what all the prep was for" and the chance to move from bootstrapped to funded, reflecting intense goal pursuit.
rationales.power: He says funding changes how "people take you seriously," how suppliers negotiate, and whether hires return calls, showing clear concern with status and influence.

rationales.achievement: What hurts is that "I did everything right and it still wasn't enough," showing his focus on performance, competence, and whether effort earns success.
Even after the term sheet, he presents himself as "focused" rather than celebratory, reflecting continued performance orientation around closing the deal.
rationales.security: He admits he was running scenarios about having to "go back to bootstrapping for another year" and felt his "shoulders drop" when the Miami call came in, showing relief at restored stability and reduced risk.
